title: My Last Duchess
content:
آ آ آ آ  FERRARA


آ آ آ آ Thatï؟½s my last Duchess painted on the wall,
آ آ آ آ Looking as if she were alive. I call
آ آ آ آ That piece a wonder, now: Frï؟½ Pandolfï؟½s hands
آ آ آ آ Worked busily a day, and there she stands.
آ آ آ آ Willï؟½t please you sit and look at her? I said
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Frï؟½ Pandolfï؟½ by design, for never read
آ آ آ آ Strangers like you that pictured countenance,
آ آ آ آ The depth and passion of its earnest glance,
آ آ آ آ But to myself they turned (since none puts by
آ آ آ آ The curtain I have drawn for you, but I)
آ آ آ آ And seemed as they would ask me, if they durst,
آ آ آ آ How such a glance came there; so, not the first
آ آ آ آ Are you to turn and ask thus. Sir, ï؟½twas not
آ آ آ آ Her husbandï؟½s presence only, called that spot
آ آ آ آ Of joy into the Duchessï؟½ cheek: perhaps
آ آ آ آ Frï؟½ Pandolf chanced to say ï؟½Her mantle laps
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Over my ladyï؟½s wrist too much,ï؟½ or ï؟½Paint
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Must never hope to reproduce the faint
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Half-flush that dies along her throat;ï؟½ such stuff
آ آ آ آ Was courtesy, she thought, and cause enough
آ آ آ آ For calling up that spot of joy. She had
آ آ آ آ A heart . . . how shall I say? . . . too soon made glad,
آ آ آ آ Too easily impressed; she liked whateï؟½er
آ آ آ آ She looked on, and her looks went everywhere.
آ آ آ آ Sir, ï؟½twas all one! My favour at her breast,
آ آ آ آ The dropping of the daylight in the West,
آ آ آ آ The bough of cherries some officious fool
آ آ آ آ Broke in the orchard for her, the white mule
آ آ آ آ She rode with round the terrace, all and each
آ آ آ آ Would draw from her alike the approving speech,
آ آ آ آ Or blush, at least. She thanked men, good; but thanked
آ آ آ آ Somehow . . . I know not how . . . as if she ranked
آ آ آ آ My gift of a nine-hundred-years-old name
آ آ آ آ With anybodyï؟½s gift. Whoï؟½d stoop to blame
آ آ آ آ This sort of trifling? Even had you skill
آ آ آ آ In speech, (which I have not), to make your will
آ آ آ آ Quite clear to such an one, and say, ï؟½Just this
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Or that in you disgusts me; here you miss,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Or there exceed the markï؟½, and if she let
آ آ آ آ Herself be lessoned so, nor plainly set
آ آ آ آ Her wits to yours, forsooth, and made excuse,
آ آ آ آ Eï؟½en then would be some stooping; and I choose
آ آ آ آ Never to stoop. Oh sir, she smiled, no doubt,
آ آ آ آ Wheneï؟½er I passed her; but who passed without
آ آ آ آ Much the same smile? This grew; I gave commands;
آ آ آ آ Then all smiles stopped together. There she stands
آ آ آ آ As if alive. Willï؟½t please you rise? Weï؟½ll meet
آ آ آ آ The company below, then. I repeat,
آ آ آ آ The Count your masterï؟½s known munificence
آ آ آ آ Is ample warrant that no just pretence
آ آ آ آ Of mine for dowry will be disallowed;
آ آ آ آ Though his fair daughterï؟½s self, as I avowed
آ آ آ آ At starting, is my object. Nay, weï؟½ll go
آ آ آ آ Together down, sir. Notice Neptune, though,
آ آ آ آ Taming a sea-horse, thought a rarity,
آ آ آ آ Which Claus of Innsbruck cast in bronze for me!
meaning:
This evocative piece by Robert Browning represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Robert Browning's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
